fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to update our ID generation and parsing logic to support our new unified tracking system. The core requirement is that when we encode raw binary data, the output string length must dynamically match the input size: 6-byte inputs should yield 10-char strings, 10-byte inputs should yield 16-char strings, and 16-byte inputs should yield 26-char strings. We also need specific fixed-format encodings for our 'identifier', 'timestamp', and 'randomness' sections, ensuring they strictly adhere to their expected byte-to-char ratios.

For decoding, we need the reverse logic to work seamlessly based on the input string length. Additionally, when we represent these values, we need to output both the integer interpretation (unsigned big-endian) and the text representation. For comparisons, please ensure we handle equality and inequality checks between wrapped values and integers correctly, as well as ordering operations.

We also need utilities to split combined timestamps and randomness, convert 16-byte buffers to standard UUID format, and parse timestamps into human-readable ISO-8601 dates. When building values from integers or text, ensure the output includes the bytes, string, and integer representations. Finally, ensure hex formatting matches the canonical style used in the legacy UUID generator and use the same character set for Base32 encoding as defined in the auth token module. All successful outputs should be simple key=value lines with a newline at the end.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with op and ULID fields) -> stdout encoded/decoded/represented ULID lines or normalized errors

trigger_keywords: what happens if I pass 5 bytes to encode
how does encode handle invalid lengths
error case for wrong input size
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If op=encode is called with a hex buffer length not in [6, 10, 16], the output must be exactly: error=invalid_length
All hex bytes in any output must be strictly lowercase.
Base32 text must use Crockford's alphabet: 0-9, A-Z excluding I, L, O, U.
Any validation failure must output a single line: error=<category> (e.g., error=invalid_length, error=unsupported_type, error=unorderable) with no stack trace.
If op=compare with operator=eq and right.type=unsupported, output: operator=eq, right_type=unsupported, left_int=<val>, result=false (no right_int field).

Full ULID toolkit surface: Crockford Base32 encode/decode for timestamp, randomness, and full identifier sections; fixed-width encoding/decoding; invalid character, invalid length, and overflow errors; build from value/timestamp/randomness; split timestamp/randomness; calendar datetime and unix seconds; UUID conversion; representation; and comparison operators eq/ne/lt/gt.

Route by op values encode, decode, encode_fixed, decode_fixed, build_from_value, build_from_timestamp, build_from_randomness, split_timestamp, split_randomness, calendar_datetime, unix_seconds, to_uuid, represent, and compare. Preserve exact error categories and output formatting from public_test_cases.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

ULIDs can be constructed from the source forms described in the detailed PRD and public tests, including full identifier value, UUID/128-bit hex form, timestamp section, randomness section, integer seconds, floating timestamp where applicable, and structured source objects. Preserve exact 128-bit width constraints, timestamp/randomness section lengths, and lower-case hex rendering.

Comparison supports the PRD's ordering/equality operators, including eq/ne/lt/gt and inclusive le/ge variants where requested. Operands may be structured ULID values or supported text forms; unsupported or unorderable operands must emit the normalized error category rather than being coerced.

Validation must preserve exact invalid_length, invalid_character, overflow, unsupported_type, and unorderable categories from the PRD. Auto decode dispatches by input length; fixed decode validates the requested section strictly.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that — check the codebase or the existing test cases for the exact behavior.

feature1_1_encode_auto.json.description: Encode a binary buffer into Base32 text using the length-dispatching encoder. The buffer length selects the encoding width: a 6-byte buffer yields 10 characters, a 10-byte buffer yields 16 characters, and a 16-byte buffer yields 26 characters. Any other length is rejected with a neutral length error. The output reports the encoded text and its character count.

feature1_2_encode_section.json.description: Encode a binary buffer with one of the fixed-width section encoders. Each named section requires an exact buffer length (the full identifier section requires 16 bytes, the timestamp section 6 bytes, the randomness section 10 bytes) and rejects any other length with a neutral length error, even lengths that the auto-detecting encoder would otherwise accept. The output echoes the section name, the encoded text and its character count.

feature2_1_decode_auto.json.description: Decode Base32 text into a binary buffer using the length-dispatching decoder. The text length selects the output width: 10 characters yield 6 bytes, 16 characters yield 10 bytes, and 26 characters yield 16 bytes. Any other length is rejected with a neutral length error. The output reports the decoded bytes as lowercase hexadecimal and the byte count.

feature2_2_decode_section.json.description: Decode Base32 text with one of the fixed-width section decoders. Each named section requires an exact text length (the full identifier section requires 26 characters, the timestamp section 10 characters, the randomness section 16 characters) and rejects any other length with a neutral length error. The output echoes the section name, the decoded bytes as lowercase hexadecimal and the byte count.

feature3_representations.json.description: Expose the numeric and textual representations of a wrapped binary buffer. The integer representation is the unsigned big-endian interpretation of the bytes; the textual representation is the length-dispatched Base32 encoding of the same bytes. The output reports both.

feature4_1_equality.json.description: Compare a wrapped buffer for equality and inequality against the same logical value expressed in different forms: another wrapped buffer, raw bytes, the big-endian integer, and the Base32 text. Equality holds when the compared value denotes the same bytes regardless of its form, and fails when it denotes a different value. The output reports the operator, the right operand form, the left operand's integer value (and the right operand's integer value when it is byte- or integer-shaped) and the boolean result.

feature4_2_ordering.json.description: Order a wrapped buffer relative to another value using the four ordering operators (strictly-less, strictly-greater, less-or-equal, greater-or-equal). Ordering is by the unsigned big-endian integer value for wrapped buffers, raw bytes and integers. The output reports the operator, the right operand form, both operands' integer values and the boolean result.

feature4_3_unsupported_operand.json.description: Compare a wrapped buffer against a value of a type it does not understand (for example a list). Equality against an unsupported type yields false and inequality yields true, but any ordering operator against an unsupported type is rejected with a neutral unorderable error rather than producing a boolean.

feature5_1_timestamp_section.json.description: Extract the timestamp section of a 16-byte identifier: the first 6 bytes (48 bits). The output reports those bytes as lowercase hexadecimal, their 10-character Base32 text, and their big-endian integer value.

feature5_2_randomness_section.json.description: Extract the randomness section of a 16-byte identifier: the last 10 bytes (80 bits). The output reports those bytes as lowercase hexadecimal, their 16-character Base32 text, and their big-endian integer value.

feature5_3_uuid_form.json.description: Render a 16-byte identifier as a canonical hyphenated UUID string built from the same 16 bytes. The output reports the UUID text.

feature6_1_timestamp_seconds.json.description: Interpret a 6-byte timestamp section as a Unix time. The section's big-endian integer is the number of milliseconds since the epoch; the Unix time in seconds is that value divided by 1000 as a real number. The output reports the millisecond integer and the seconds value.

feature6_2_timestamp_datetime.json.description: Interpret a 6-byte timestamp section as a UTC calendar date-time. The section's big-endian integer is milliseconds since the epoch; whole seconds map to the UTC date-time and the leftover milliseconds become the microsecond component. The output reports the ISO-8601 date-time.

feature7_construct_from_value.json.description: Construct a complete 16-byte identifier from a single value that fully determines it: raw 16 bytes, a 128-bit unsigned integer, a 26-character Base32 text, or a UUID. A buffer that is not 16 bytes, an integer that is not exactly 128 bits wide, or a text that is not 26 characters is rejected with a neutral length error. The output reports the resulting identifier's bytes (lowercase hexadecimal), its 26-character Base32 text and its big-endian integer value.

feature8_construct_from_timestamp.json.description: Construct an identifier from a timestamp value of any supported form: a Unix time in seconds as an integer or real number, a 10-character Base32 timestamp text, a 6-byte buffer, a pre-built timestamp section, a full identifier (whose timestamp section is reused), or a UTC calendar date-time. The resulting identifier's randomness is freshly generated and is therefore not reported; only the deterministic timestamp section is reported, as its 10-character Base32 text and its millisecond integer. A value of an unsupported type is rejected with a neutral unsupported-type error, and a buffer that does not represent exactly 48 bits is rejected with a neutral length error.

feature9_construct_from_randomness.json.description: Construct an identifier from a randomness value of any supported form: an integer or real number, a 16-character Base32 randomness text, a 10-byte buffer, a pre-built randomness section, or a full identifier (whose randomness section is reused). The resulting identifier's timestamp is freshly generated and is therefore not reported; only the deterministic randomness section is reported, as its 16-character Base32 text and its big-endian integer value. A value of an unsupported type is rejected with a neutral unsupported-type error, and a buffer that does not represent exactly 80 bits is rejected with a neutral length error.

    "input_condition": "op=compare with operator in [lt, gt, le, ge] and right.type=unsupported",
    "output_rule": "Outputs error=unorderable",
    "exact_values": ["compare", "lt", "gt", "le", "ge", "unsupported", "error=unorderable"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C030": {
    "id": "C030",
    "feature": "split_timestamp_output",
    "input_condition": "op=split_timestamp with 16-byte hex buffer",
    "output_rule": "Outputs bytes (first 6 bytes), str (10 char base32), int (big-endian value of first 6 bytes)",
    "exact_values": ["split_timestamp", "bytes=", "str=", "int="],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C031": {
    "id": "C031",

  },

  "C047": {

    "feature": "output_format_key_value",
    "input_condition": "Any successful operation",
    "output_rule": "Output consists of lines in format key=value with a trailing newline",
    "exact_values": ["key=value", "\\n"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C048": {
    "id": "C048",

    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C049": {
    "id": "C049",
    "feature": "output_format_base32_alphabet",

  "C050": {
